1:
"And Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, took either of them his censer, and put fire therein, and put incense thereon, and offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not." The witnesses note the strange fire of Nadab and Abihu — the crux of unauthorized worship. On "Nadab and Abihu... offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not," interpreters expound the sin. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the strange fire — "offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not": Nadab and Abihu (Aaron's two eldest sons) took their censers, put fire and incense, and offered "strange fire" before the LORD, fire he had not commanded; the witnesses note the significance — the strange fire (common fire, not from the altar's holy fire kindled from heaven, Leviticus 9:24; 6:13, or incense offered at a wrong time or way, in any case unauthorized, "which he commanded them not"); the lesson — the sin of will-worship and unauthorized worship (the offering of what God had not commanded, presuming to worship God in their own way and not his, the great sin of adding to or altering God's appointed worship, "in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men," Matthew 15:9; Colossians 2:23, will-worship); the gravity (the worship not commanded is forbidden, "which he commanded them not" — God's silence not license but prohibition in worship, the regulative principle, Deuteronomy 12:32, "what thing soever I command you, observe to do it: thou shalt not add thereto, nor diminish from it"); the figure — the warning against all will-worship and human invention in the worship of God; some add the likelihood they were drunk (vv.9, the prohibition of wine following).

On "took either of them his censer, and put fire therein, and put incense thereon," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the act. Wesley and Ellicott note the censers, the fire, the incense (the act of presumption, the unauthorized offering) — the presumption (the priests acting of their own will, not by command); the figure (the will-worship, the human addition).

On the strange fire, the grammatical-historical note the unauthorized: strange fire, which he commanded them not — the worship not commanded; the providence (the sin of unauthorized worship, the judgment to follow, vv.2). The verse displays the strange fire of Nadab and Abihu.

The witnesses agree the substance: Nadab and Abihu offered strange fire which God had not commanded (Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of unauthorized worship: "offered strange fire before the LORD, which he commanded them not" — the sin of will-worship and unauthorized worship (the offering of what God had not commanded, worshipping God in man's way and not his, Matthew 15:9; Colossians 2:23), God's silence in worship a prohibition not a license (Deuteronomy 12:32, neither adding nor diminishing), the warning against all human invention in the worship of God. The verse displays the strange-fire crux. The verse marks the fire: strange. The verse displays the worship: unauthorized. The verse displays the command: not given. The verse displays the will-worship: condemned. The fire displays the sin of unauthorized worship. The verse displays the fire: strange. The verse displays the worship: presumptuous. The verse displays the warning: against invention. The strange fire of Nadab and Abihu displays the crux: strange fire, which he commanded them not (Matthew 15:9; Colossians 2:23; Deuteronomy 12:32) — the sin of will-worship, worshipping God in man's way and not his, the warning against all human invention in worship. The verse displays the fire of judgment (v.2).
2:
"And there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them, and they died before the LORD." The witnesses note the fire of judgment devouring Nadab and Abihu. On "there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them, and they died before the LORD," interpreters expound the judgment. The reformed and the dominant reading note the fire — "there went out fire from the LORD, and devoured them": the same fire that had just consumed the offering in acceptance (Leviticus 9:24) now went out from the LORD and devoured Nadab and Abihu, and they died; the witnesses note the significance — the swift, severe judgment on the unauthorized worship (the fire of God's holiness, which accepted the right offering, now destroying the false; the same fire of God a savour of life to the obedient and of death to the presumptuous); the lesson — the severity of God's holiness against the profaning of his worship (the awful judgment, the warning that God will not be mocked nor worshipped in man's way; the fire that sanctifies and the fire that consumes, Hebrews 12:29, "our God is a consuming fire"); the gravity (the judgment swift, public, at the very inauguration of the priesthood, a warning to all); the figure — the holiness of God that consumes the profane, the seriousness of approaching God wrongly (the warning of 1 Corinthians 11:30, the judgment on unworthy approach; the fear of God in worship).

On "and they died before the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the death. Wesley and JFB note the death of Nadab and Abihu before the LORD (the swift judgment, the bodies consumed yet the garments perhaps untouched, vv.5) — the severity (the judgment on the profane); the figure (the death the wages of presuming on God's holiness). The verse displays the fire of judgment devouring Nadab and Abihu.

The witnesses agree the substance: fire from the LORD devoured Nadab and Abihu, and they died (Wesley, JFB, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the fire of judgment — the same fire that accepted the offering (Leviticus 9:24) now consuming the presumptuous, the severe judgment on unauthorized worship, the severity of God's holiness against the profaning of his worship ("our God is a consuming fire," Hebrews 12:29), the warning at the very inauguration of the priesthood, a figure of the seriousness of approaching God wrongly. The verse displays the judgment. The verse marks the fire: from the LORD. The verse displays the devouring: of the presumptuous. The verse displays the holiness: consuming. The verse displays the warning: severe. The fire displays the judgment on unauthorized worship. The verse displays the fire: consuming. The verse displays the death: swift. The verse displays the holiness: severe. The fire of judgment devouring Nadab and Abihu displays the severity of God's holiness: fire from the LORD devoured them, and they died (Leviticus 9:24; Hebrews 12:29) — the same fire that accepted the offering now consuming the profane, the warning against approaching God wrongly. The verse displays Moses' word of God's holiness (v.3).
3:
"Then Moses said unto Aaron, This is it that the LORD spake, saying, I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified. And Aaron held his peace." The witnesses note God sanctified in them that come nigh, and Aaron's silence — the crux of God's holiness vindicated. On "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified," interpreters expound the word. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the sanctifying — "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and before all the people I will be glorified": Moses explained the judgment by God's word, that he would be sanctified (treated as holy) in those who draw near to him (the priests), and glorified before all the people; the witnesses note the significance — God's holiness vindicated (the more privileged the nearness, the greater the holiness required; those who come nigh to God, especially the priests, must treat him as holy, or he will vindicate his holiness in judgment); the lesson — God will be sanctified, one way or another (either by the reverent obedience of those who draw near, or by judgment on their profaneness; "be not deceived; God is not mocked," Galatians 6:7; the nearer to God, the holier the requirement, "judgment must begin at the house of God," 1 Peter 4:17); the glorifying (God glorified before the people by the judgment, his holiness made known); the figure — the holiness of God that must be honored, the reverence due in worship and in those who minister.

On "And Aaron held his peace," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note Aaron's silence. Wesley and Henry note that Aaron "held his peace" (was silent) — the silence of submission (Aaron, the bereaved father, silent in submission to God's righteous judgment, neither murmuring nor complaining, accepting the justice of God, like Job, "the LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD," Job 1:21; Psalm 39:9, "I was dumb, I opened not my mouth; because thou didst it"); the figure (the silent submission to God's holy will, the resignation of faith). The verse displays God sanctified in them that come nigh, and Aaron's silence.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses declared God's word that he would be sanctified in those who come nigh, and Aaron held his peace (Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's holiness vindicated: "I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me" — God's holiness must be honored, especially by those who draw near (the priests), and he will be sanctified either by their reverence or by judgment on their profaneness (1 Peter 4:17), the nearer to God the holier the requirement; and Aaron's silence the model of submission to God's righteous judgment (Job 1:21; Psalm 39:9), the resignation of faith. The verse displays the holiness-vindicated crux. The verse marks the sanctifying: of God. The verse displays the nearness: the holiness. The verse displays the Aaron: silent. The verse displays the submission: of faith. The word displays God's holiness vindicated. The verse displays the God: sanctified. The verse displays the holiness: required. The verse displays the Aaron: submitting. God sanctified in them that come nigh, and Aaron's silence, display the crux: I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me, and Aaron held his peace (1 Peter 4:17; Job 1:21; Psalm 39:9) — God's holiness vindicated, the holier requirement of those who draw near, and the silent submission of faith to God's righteous judgment. The verse displays the carrying out of the bodies (vv.4-5).
4:
"And Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel the uncle of Aaron, and said unto them, Come near, carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp." The very brief verse notes the cousins carrying out the bodies. The grammatical voices note the removal. Clarke and Gill note that "Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel the uncle of Aaron... Come near, carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp" — Moses called the cousins of Nadab and Abihu (sons of Uzziel, Aaron's uncle) to carry the bodies out of the camp; the cousins (the kinsmen, not the priests, removing the dead); the carrying out (the bodies removed from the sanctuary, the dead not to defile the holy place); the out of the camp (the bodies taken outside, the place of the dead); the figure (the removal of the dead from the sanctuary, the holiness guarded; the priests not to defile themselves, vv.6-7).

On "Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan," the witnesses note the kinsmen. Gill notes the cousins called (not the priests, who were not to touch the dead, vv.6) — the removal (the kinsmen carrying out the bodies).

On "carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the holiness: "carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp" — the bodies removed from the sanctuary (the holiness guarded, the dead not to defile the holy place); the figure (the holiness of the sanctuary, the dead removed); the providence (the bodies carried out, the priests kept from defilement, vv.6-7). The verse displays the cousins carrying out the bodies.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the removal — "Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan... carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp," the removal (Clarke, Gill); "Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan," the kinsmen (Gill); "carry your brethren from before the sanctuary out of the camp," the holiness (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the carried bodies. The verse marks the cousins: called. The verse displays the bodies: carried out. The verse displays the sanctuary: kept holy. The verse displays the dead: removed. The removal displays the holiness of the sanctuary guarded. The verse displays the kinsmen: carrying. The verse displays the bodies: removed. The verse displays the holiness: guarded. The cousins carrying out the bodies display the holiness guarded: carry your brethren out of the camp — the kinsmen removing the dead from the sanctuary, the holiness of the holy place guarded. The verse displays the bodies in their coats (v.5).
5:
"So they went near, and carried them in their coats out of the camp; as Moses had said." The very brief verse notes the bodies carried out in their coats. The grammatical voices note the coats. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they went near, and carried them in their coats out of the camp; as Moses had said" — the cousins carried the bodies out in their coats (the priestly tunics), as Moses said; the in their coats (the bodies still in their priestly garments, the fire having consumed their lives but not their clothes); the carried out (removed from the camp); the as Moses said (the obedience); the figure (the fire devouring the men but not their garments, the strange and awful judgment; the bodies removed in their coats).

On "they went near, and carried them in their coats," the witnesses note the coats. Gill notes the bodies in their coats (the priestly tunics, the fire having taken their lives but not their clothes, the awful judgment) — the wonder (the fire consuming the men, not the garments).

On "out of the camp; as Moses had said," the grammatical-historical note the obedience: "out of the camp; as Moses had said" — the bodies removed as commanded (the obedience); the figure (the holiness guarded); the providence (the bodies carried out, the judgment's marks). The verse displays the bodies carried out in their coats.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the coats — "they went near, and carried them in their coats out of the camp; as Moses had said," the coats (Keil, Gill); "they went near, and carried them in their coats," the coats (Gill); "out of the camp; as Moses had said," the obedience (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the carried bodies. The verse marks the bodies: in their coats. The verse displays the carried: out of the camp. The verse displays the judgment: awful. The verse displays the obedience: as said. The carrying displays the bodies removed as commanded. The verse displays the coats: untouched. The verse displays the bodies: removed. The verse displays the obedience: kept. The bodies carried out in their coats display the awful judgment: carried in their coats out of the camp — the bodies still in their priestly garments, the fire having consumed the men but not their coats, removed as Moses said. The verse displays the priests forbidden to mourn (vv.6-7).
6:
"And Moses said unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons, Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die, and lest wrath come upon all the people: but let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled." The witnesses note the priests forbidden to mourn for the slain. On "Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Clarke and Gill note that "Moses said unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar... Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die, and lest wrath come upon all the people" — Aaron and his surviving sons were forbidden to show the usual signs of mourning (uncovering the head, rending the clothes) for Nadab and Abihu, on pain of death; the prohibition of mourning (the priests not to mourn in the customary way); the reason (the priests consecrated, in the midst of their ministry, not to defile their consecration with mourning, or to seem to dispute God's righteous judgment); the lest ye die (the severity, the priests to submit, not to mourn the justly judged).

On "lest ye die, and lest wrath come upon all the people," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the submission. Calvin and Ellicott note the priests not to mourn (lest they die, lest wrath come on the people) — the lesson (the priests to submit to God's judgment, not to dispute it by mourning; the consecration not to be interrupted; the acknowledgment of the justice of God's judgment, even by the bereaved).

On "let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the people's mourning: "let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled" — the people (not the priests) might lament the judgment (the public grief permitted to the congregation); the figure (the priests submitting, the people mourning); the providence (the priests in their ministry, the people bewailing). The verse displays the priests forbidden to mourn for the slain.

The witnesses agree the substance: the priests were forbidden to mourn, though the people might bewail the judgment (Clarke, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the priests forbidden to mourn — Aaron and his sons not to show the signs of mourning for the slain, lest they die (the priests consecrated, in their ministry, not to interrupt their consecration nor seem to dispute God's righteous judgment, but to submit, acknowledging its justice), while the people might bewail the judgment; the figure of submission to God's holy judgment even in bereavement. The verse displays the prohibition of mourning. The verse marks the mourning: forbidden. The verse displays the priests: not to mourn. The verse displays the submission: to the judgment. The verse displays the people: bewailing. The prohibition displays the priests' submission to God's judgment. The verse displays the priests: submitting. The verse displays the people: mourning. The verse displays the judgment: just. The priests forbidden to mourn for the slain display the submission: uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes — the priests not to mourn, submitting to God's righteous judgment, while the people bewail the burning. The verse displays the priests not to leave the sanctuary (v.7).
7:
"And ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses." The very brief verse notes the priests kept at the sanctuary by the anointing. The grammatical voices note the prohibition. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses" — the priests were not to leave the door of the tabernacle (to follow the dead, or mourn), lest they die, because the anointing oil of the LORD was upon them; the not going out (the priests to remain at their ministry, not to leave for the funeral); the the anointing oil upon them (the consecration, the priests set apart, not to be interrupted by mourning); the lest ye die (the severity, the consecration to be honored); the figure (the consecration of the priests, the claim of God's service above natural ties; the obedience of the priests).

On "ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle... lest ye die," the witnesses note the consecration. Gill notes the priests kept at the sanctuary (not leaving for the dead, the consecration, vv.6) — the consecration (the priests set apart for God's service).

On "for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the obedience: "for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses" — the anointing oil (the consecration, the claim of God's service); the obedience (the priests doing as Moses said, submitting); the figure (the consecration above natural ties, the obedience of submission, Matthew 8:22, "let the dead bury their dead; but go thou and preach"); the providence (the priests at their ministry, the obedience). The verse displays the priests kept at the sanctuary by the anointing.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the consecration — "ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle... for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses," the consecration and obedience (Keil, Gill); "ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle... lest ye die," the consecration (Gill); "for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses," the obedience (Matthew 8:22, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the consecrated priests. The verse marks the priests: kept at the door. The verse displays the anointing: upon them. The verse displays the consecration: honored. The verse displays the obedience: kept. The consecration displays God's service above natural ties. The verse displays the priests: remaining. The verse displays the anointing: the claim. The verse displays the obedience: shown. The priests kept at the sanctuary by the anointing display the consecration: ye shall not go out, for the anointing oil is upon you (Matthew 8:22) — the priests kept at their ministry by their consecration, the claim of God's service above natural ties, the obedience of submission. The verse displays the prohibition of wine (vv.8-11).
8:
"And the LORD spake unto Aaron, saying," The very brief verse notes the LORD speaking directly to Aaron concerning wine. The grammatical voices note the speaking. Keil and Delitzsch and Henry note that "the LORD spake unto Aaron, saying" — the LORD spoke directly to Aaron (a rare honor, usually through Moses), now to give the prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry (vv.9-11); the speaking to Aaron (the LORD addressing Aaron directly, perhaps to comfort and honor him after his loss and submission); the occasion (after the death of his sons, the prohibition of wine, perhaps suggesting Nadab and Abihu had been drunk); the figure (God's direct word to Aaron, the honor and the instruction). That the LORD spoke directly to Aaron, who had just lost his sons and held his peace, shows God's tender regard for the submissive, honoring the bereaved father with his own word.

On "the LORD spake unto Aaron," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the direct word. Henry and Keil note the LORD speaking directly to Aaron (a mark of honor, usually through Moses) — the honor (God addressing Aaron, perhaps comforting and honoring him after his submission).

On the speaking, the grammatical-historical note the command: the LORD spake unto Aaron — the prohibition of wine introduced; the providence (God instructing Aaron, the sober priesthood, vv.9-11). The verse displays the LORD speaking directly to Aaron concerning wine.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the speaking — "the LORD spake unto Aaron, saying," the direct word (vv.9-11, Keil, Henry); "the LORD spake unto Aaron," the direct word (Henry); the command (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the speaking LORD. The verse marks the speaking: to Aaron. The verse displays the honor: the direct word. The verse displays the prohibition: of wine. The verse displays the comfort: to the bereaved. The speaking displays God's direct word to Aaron. The verse displays the LORD: speaking. The verse displays the Aaron: addressed. The verse displays the instruction: given. The verse displays the prohibition of wine (vv.9-11).
9:
"Do not drink wine nor strong drink, thou, nor thy sons with thee, when ye go into the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations:" The witnesses note the prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry. On "Do not drink wine nor strong drink... when ye go into the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Calvin and Gill note that "do not drink wine nor strong drink, thou, nor thy sons with thee, when ye go into the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: it shall be a statute for ever" — the priests were forbidden to drink wine or strong drink when they went in to minister, on pain of death, a perpetual statute; the prohibition (no wine or strong drink in the ministry); the lest ye die (the severity, the sobriety required); the perpetual statute (the standing rule for the priests); the figure (the sobriety of those who minister, the clear mind required in God's service; perhaps Nadab and Abihu had been drunk, the connection with their sin).

On "Do not drink wine nor strong drink... when ye go into the tabernacle," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the sobriety. Calvin and Keil note the priests to be sober in their ministry (no wine, the clear mind, the reverence) — the lesson (the sobriety required in God's service, the danger of drunkenness in worship; the warning, perhaps from Nadab and Abihu's sin); the figure (the sober, watchful service, 1 Peter 5:8; the minister sober, 1 Timothy 3:2-3).

On "it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: "it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations" — the perpetual statute (the standing rule); the figure (the sobriety of God's servants, the clear mind in worship); the providence (the sober priesthood, the perpetual rule). The verse displays the prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry.

The witnesses agree the substance: the priests were forbidden wine in their ministry, a perpetual statute (Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight: the prohibition of wine — the priests to be sober when they minister, on pain of death (the clear mind and reverence required in God's service, the danger of drunkenness in worship, perhaps the very sin of Nadab and Abihu), a perpetual statute, a figure of the sobriety and watchfulness required of God's servants (1 Peter 5:8; 1 Timothy 3:2-3). The verse displays the wine-prohibition. The verse marks the wine: forbidden. The verse displays the ministry: sober. The verse displays the mind: clear. The verse displays the reverence: required. The prohibition displays the sobriety required in God's service. The verse displays the priests: sober. The verse displays the wine: forbidden. The verse displays the service: reverent. The prohibition of wine for the priests in their ministry displays the sobriety required: drink no wine when ye go into the tabernacle, lest ye die (1 Peter 5:8; 1 Timothy 3:2-3) — the priests to be sober in God's service, the clear mind and reverence required, the sobriety of God's servants. The verse displays the priest's task of discernment (vv.10-11).
10:
"And that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean;" The witnesses note the priest's task of distinguishing holy from unholy — the crux of discernment. On "that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean," interpreters expound the task. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the discernment — "that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean": one reason for the sobriety (vv.9) was that the priests might discern between holy and unholy, clean and unclean; the witnesses note the significance — the priest's task of discernment (the priests to distinguish the holy from the common, the clean from the unclean, to judge and teach the difference, which a drunken mind could not do); the lesson — the discernment required in God's service (the clear mind to distinguish the holy from the profane, the office of the priest to discern and teach the difference, Ezekiel 44:23, "they shall teach my people the difference between the holy and profane, and cause them to discern between the unclean and the clean"); the figure — the spiritual discernment of God's people (the believer to discern, to try the spirits, to distinguish good and evil, Hebrews 5:14, "to discern both good and evil"; 1 Thessalonians 5:21, "prove all things; hold fast that which is good"); the sober, discerning mind required in the things of God.

On "between holy and unholy," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the holy. Wesley and Ellicott note the distinguishing of holy and unholy (the priest's discernment of the sacred) — the discernment (the holy distinguished from the common).

On "between unclean and clean," the grammatical-historical note the clean: "between unclean and clean" — the distinguishing of clean and unclean (the ceremonial discernment, the laws of clean and unclean, chs. 11-15); the figure (the discernment of the holy and the clean); the providence (the priest's task to discern and teach, vv.11; the sober mind for discernment). The verse displays the priest's task of distinguishing holy from unholy.

The witnesses agree the substance: the priests were to discern between holy and unholy, clean and unclean (Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of discernment: "that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean" — the priest's task of discernment (to distinguish the holy from the common and teach the difference, Ezekiel 44:23), requiring the sober, clear mind (vv.9), a figure of the spiritual discernment required of God's people (Hebrews 5:14; 1 Thessalonians 5:21), the trying of all things and distinguishing of good and evil. The verse displays the teaching of the statutes (v.11).
11:
"And that ye may teach the children of Israel all the statutes which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses." The very brief verse notes the priest's task of teaching the statutes. The grammatical voices note the teaching. Ellicott and Gill note that "that ye may teach the children of Israel all the statutes which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses" — another task of the priests (requiring the sober mind, vv.9) was to teach Israel all the statutes God had given by Moses; the teaching (the priests to instruct the people in God's law); the all the statutes (the whole law, the ordinances); the by the hand of Moses (the law given through Moses); the figure (the priest's office to teach, the ministry of the word, Malachi 2:7, "the priest's lips should keep knowledge, and they should seek the law at his mouth").

On "that ye may teach the children of Israel all the statutes," the witnesses note the teaching. Gill notes the priests to teach the law (the office of instruction, Deuteronomy 33:10) — the figure (the priest the teacher, the ministry of the word, Malachi 2:7).

On "which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses," the grammatical-historical note the law: "which the LORD hath spoken unto them by the hand of Moses" — the law given through Moses (the whole law to be taught); the figure (the ministry of the word, the teaching office); the providence (the priests teaching the law, the sober mind required, vv.9). The verse displays the priest's task of teaching the statutes.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 12:

"Wherefore have ye not eaten the sin offering in the holy place, seeing it is most holy, and God hath given it you to bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD?"
